era_context: The French Revolution is in its radical phase, with the Reign of Terror having ended in July 1794. The French Republic is consolidating power under the Directory (1795-1799). Europe is experiencing revolutionary upheaval and counter-revolutionary responses. The Industrial Revolution continues to accelerate in Britain. The Americas are establishing new republics and consolidating independence.

events.title: Treaty of Basel between France and Prussia
events.description: On 5 April 1795, France and Prussia sign the Treaty of Basel, ending Prussian participation in the War of the First Coalition against France. The treaty recognizes French control of the left bank of the Rhine and provides for Prussian neutrality in the ongoing war. The treaty is a significant diplomatic victory for France, as it removes one of the major powers opposing the Revolution. Prussia's withdrawal from the coalition reflects the exhaustion of the Prussian treasury and the desire of King Frederick William II to avoid further military commitments. The treaty effectively splits the anti-French coalition and allows France to concentrate its forces against Austria and other remaining enemies.
key_figures: Prussian diplomat Karl August von Hardenberg

sources.name: Treaty text and official French and Prussian records

events.certainty_note: The treaty and its date are well-documented in French and Prussian archives. The terms and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)
Led to 1795-002 (Treaty of Basel with Spain)

events.title: Treaty of Basel between France and Spain
events.description: On 22 July 1795, France and Spain sign the Treaty of Basel, ending Spanish participation in the War of the First Coalition. The treaty provides for the cession of the Spanish portion of Santo Domingo (present-day Dominican Republic) to France and the restoration of Spanish territories captured by France. The treaty reflects Spain's military exhaustion and the desire of King Charles IV to end the costly war. Like the Prussian treaty, the Spanish treaty further weakens the anti-French coalition. However, the treaty also sets the stage for the Franco-Spanish alliance of 1796, as Spain seeks French support against Britain.

sources.name: Treaty text and official French and Spanish records

events.certainty_note: The treaty and its date are well-documented in French and Spanish archives. The terms and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)

events.title: Partitions of Poland: Third Partition completed
events.description: In 1795, the Third Partition of Poland is completed, with Russia, Prussia, and Austria dividing the remaining Polish territories among themselves. The final partition, signed in October 1795, eliminates Poland as an independent state. The partitions (1772, 1793, 1795) represent one of the most significant territorial redistributions of the 18th century and demonstrate the power of the great powers to reshape the map of Europe. Poland ceases to exist as a sovereign state until 1918. The partitions are condemned by Polish patriots and become a rallying point for Polish nationalism throughout the 19th century.

sources.name: Treaty texts and official Russian, Prussian, and Austrian records
Russian, Prussian, and Austrian diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The Third Partition of Poland in 1795 is well-documented in Russian, Prussian, and Austrian archives. The date and terms are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader history of Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (1569-1795)

events.title: Reign of Terror ends; Thermidorian Reaction consolidates
events.description: By 1795, the Thermidorian Reaction (which began in July 1794 with the fall of Robespierre) has consolidated its control over the French government. The radical phase of the Revolution has ended, and the Directory (1795-1799) is establishing a more conservative, bourgeois republic. The execution of Robespierre and the closure of the Committee of Public Safety mark the end of the Terror. In 1795, the Directory suppresses both radical Jacobin uprisings (the Conspiracy of the Equals, led by Gracchus Babeuf) and royalist uprisings (the Vendée uprising). The Directory's consolidation represents a shift from revolutionary idealism toward pragmatic governance and the protection of property rights.

sources.name: French government records and Directory documents
Personal correspondence and memoirs of Directory members

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of the Thermidorian Reaction by 1795 is well-documented in French government records and contemporary sources. The date and significance are uncontested.

events.title: British naval victory at the Glorious First of June (1794) and continued naval dominance
events.description: By 1795, the British Royal Navy has consolidated its dominance following the Glorious First of June (1794), a major naval battle against the French. In 1795, the British continue to dominate the seas, protecting British trade and threatening French commerce. The British navy's superiority is reflected in the capture of French colonies and the disruption of French trade. This naval dominance is a crucial factor in Britain's ability to sustain the war against France despite the military successes of the French army on land. The naval dominance also allows Britain to project power globally and to protect its expanding colonial empire.

sources.name: British Admiralty records and official dispatches

events.certainty_note: British naval dominance in 1795 is well-documented in Admiralty records. The strategic significance is uncontested.
cross_references: Led to continued British naval supremacy throughout the Napoleonic Wars

events.title: Jay Treaty between the United States and Britain
events.description: On 19 November 1794, the Jay Treaty is signed between the United States and Britain (ratified in 1795). The treaty, negotiated by Chief Justice John Jay, resolves several outstanding disputes between the two nations, including British impressment of American sailors, British support for Native American resistance to American expansion, and trade issues. The treaty is controversial in the United States, with many viewing it as too favorable to Britain. However, it averts a potential war between the two nations and establishes a framework for peaceful coexistence. The treaty also opens British markets to American trade and provides for the evacuation of British troops from American territory. The treaty is ratified by the Senate in June 1795.

sources.name: Treaty text and official U.S. and British records
U.S. Senate records and ratification documents
Contemporary American newspapers and political writings

events.certainty_note: The Jay Treaty and its ratification in 1795 are well-documented in U.S. and British archives. The date and terms are uncontested.

events.title: Kościuszko Uprising in Poland
events.description: In 1794 (not 1795, but the aftermath continues into 1795), Polish general Tadeusz Kościuszko leads an uprising against Russian and Prussian occupation. The uprising, which begins in March 1794, is suppressed by October 1794, but its aftermath and the memory of the uprising dominate Polish consciousness in 1795. Kościuszko is captured and imprisoned, and the uprising's failure contributes to the final partition of Poland in 1795. The uprising represents the last major Polish resistance to partition and becomes a symbol of Polish nationalism and independence.
key_figures: Tadeusz Kościuszko (Polish general and uprising leader)
events.certainty_note: The Kościuszko Uprising occurred in 1794, but its aftermath and significance extend into 1795. The dates and outcomes are well-documented.

events.title: British capture of the Cape of Good Hope (preliminary operations)
events.region: Cape of Good Hope, South Africa
events.description: In 1795, British naval forces begin operations against the Dutch-held Cape of Good Hope. The British, seeking to deny the French access to this strategic point and to secure their own eastern trade routes, dispatch an expedition. The actual capture occurs in September 1796, but the planning and preliminary operations occur in 1795. The British recognize the strategic importance of the Cape as a supply station on the route to Asia and as a base for operations in the Indian Ocean.
key_figures: Admiral Sir George Elphinstone (British naval commander)
General James Henry Craig (British military commander)

sources.name: British Admiralty and War Office records

events.certainty_note: The planning and preliminary operations for the capture of the Cape occur in 1795, but the actual capture occurs in 1796. The dates are somewhat fluid depending on the source.
cross_references: Led to 1796-004 (British capture of the Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Qianlong Emperor's reign continues
events.description: In 1795, the Qianlong Emperor continues his reign over the Qing Dynasty. By 1795, Qianlong has ruled for 60 years and is in his 85th year of life. He is beginning to prepare for his abdication, which will occur in 1796. The Qing Dynasty is at the height of its territorial extent, having conquered Tibet, Xinjiang, and Mongolia. However, by 1795, signs of decline are beginning to appear: corruption in the bureaucracy, the rise of the eunuch Heshen, and growing social unrest. The Qing economy is beginning to show signs of strain, and the population is growing faster than agricultural production.

sources.name: Qing imperial records (Qing Shilu) and court documents
Chinese historical chronicles and official histories
Contemporary European diplomatic reports from China

events.certainty_note: The continuation of Qianlong's reign in 1795 is well-documented in Qing imperial records. The date and circumstances are uncontested.

events.title: British East India Company consolidates control in India
events.description: By 1795, the British East India Company has consolidated its control over much of India, particularly in Bengal and the Madras Presidency. The Company's military victories over Indian rulers and French competitors have established British dominance. In 1795, the Company is expanding its administrative control and extracting revenue from Indian territories. The Company's rule is characterized by the subordination of Indian interests to British commercial interests and the extraction of wealth from India. The Company's expansion sets the stage for the formal British Raj, which will be established in 1858.

sources.name: British East India Company records and correspondence

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of British East India Company control in 1795 is well-documented in Company records and British government correspondence. The date and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader British expansion in India

events.title: Haitian Revolution: Toussaint Louverture consolidates power
events.description: In 1795, Toussaint Louverture, a former enslaved person who has risen to prominence in the Haitian Revolution, consolidates his power over the island of Saint-Domingue (present-day Haiti). The Haitian Revolution, which began in 1791, has resulted in the abolition of slavery and the establishment of a Black republic. By 1795, Toussaint has emerged as the dominant military and political leader. He unifies the various factions of the revolution and begins to establish a stable government. Toussaint's rise represents one of the most significant challenges to European colonialism and slavery in the Americas.
key_figures: Toussaint Louverture (Haitian military and political leader)
events.certainty_note: Toussaint Louverture's consolidation of power in 1795 is well-documented in French colonial records and contemporary accounts. The date and significance are uncontested.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Russian advances continue
events.description: In 1795, Russian forces continue their advances against the Ottoman Empire, particularly in the Caucasus region. The Russo-Turkish Wars (1768-1774, 1787-1792) have established Russian dominance in the region. By 1795, Russia is consolidating its control over the northern Caucasus and the Black Sea region. The Ottoman Empire is in decline, and Russia is the ascendant power in the region. These conflicts set the stage for the 19th-century 'Eastern Question,' which will dominate European diplomacy.
key_figures: General Grigory Potemkin (deceased 1791, but his legacy continues)
events.certainty_note: The broad outlines of Russian-Ottoman conflict in 1795 are well-documented, though specific battles and dates may vary slightly in different sources.
cross_references: Led to Russian consolidation in the Caucasus

events.title: Whiskey Rebellion: Federal suppression and consolidation of U.S. authority
events.description: In 1795, the aftermath of the Whiskey Rebellion (1794) is being consolidated. Federal troops, having suppressed the rebellion in 1794, remain in the region to enforce federal authority. The rebellion's suppression demonstrates the federal government's ability to enforce its laws and taxation. By 1795, the federal government has successfully established its authority over the frontier regions. The rebellion's suppression marks a turning point in American federalism, establishing the principle that the federal government, not state governments or local militias, holds ultimate coercive power.
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of federal authority following the rebellion is well-documented. 1795 marks the point at which the rebellion's lessons had been absorbed into American political practice.
cross_references: Led to 1796-005 (Consolidation of U.S. federal authority)

events.title: British capture of French colonies in the Caribbean
events.description: In 1795, British naval and military forces capture several French colonies in the Caribbean, including Guadeloupe and Martinique. These captures are part of the broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797) and reflect British naval dominance. The captures disrupt French trade and weaken French colonial power. However, many of these colonies are returned to France in subsequent peace treaties. The captures demonstrate the importance of naval power in colonial conflicts and the vulnerability of French colonies to British attack.

sources.name: British Admiralty and War Office records

events.certainty_note: The British captures of French colonies in 1795 are well-documented in British and French records. The dates and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)

events.title: Mungo Park's exploration preparations and early travels
events.description: In 1795, Scottish explorer Mungo Park, sponsored by the African Association, begins his preparations for his expedition to explore the Niger River in West Africa. Park travels from the Gambia River inland in 1795, beginning his journey that will lead to the discovery of the Niger River in July 1796. Park's expedition is significant for European geographical knowledge of Africa and marks the beginning of European penetration into the interior that will lead to colonization.

sources.name: Mungo Park's journal and later published account 'Travels in the Interior Districts of Africa' (1799)
Contemporary European geographical and travel literature

events.certainty_note: Park's expedition and the dates are well-documented in his journal and published account. The route and discoveries are uncontested.
cross_references: Led to 1796-013 (Mungo Park reaches the Niger River)

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above have contested elements: (1) The Treaty of Basel with Prussia is sometimes dated as 5 April 1795, but some sources cite different dates depending on the calendar system used (Julian vs. Gregorian). The significance of the treaty in weakening the anti-French coalition is generally accepted, but some historians argue that Prussia's withdrawal was inevitable due to financial exhaustion rather than French diplomatic skill. (2) The Treaty of Basel with Spain is sometimes dated as 22 July 1795, but the exact date varies in different sources. The treaty's significance in ending Spanish participation in the war is uncontested, but the terms regarding Santo Domingo are sometimes disputed. (3) The Third Partition of Poland is sometimes dated as occurring in 1795, but the exact date of the final partition agreement varies in different sources (some cite October 1795, others cite different dates). The fact of the partition is uncontested, but the precise date is somewhat fluid. (4) The Kościuszko Uprising occurred in 1794, not 1795, but its aftermath and significance extend into 1795. The inclusion of this event in 1795 is somewhat anachronistic. (5) The British capture of the Cape of Good Hope is sometimes dated as occurring in 1795, but the actual capture occurred in September 1796. The planning and preliminary operations occurred in 1795, but the actual capture is a 1796 event.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1795 is rich and well-documented, particularly for Europe and the Americas. European sources (British, French, Spanish, Russian, Prussian) are abundant and detailed. Asian sources (Chinese, Indian) are also available but are often filtered through European interpretations. African sources are sparse, with most information about Mungo Park's expedition coming from his own account rather than from African perspectives. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are less well-represented. There is a significant bias toward 'great men' and elite decision-makers; the experiences of ordinary people, enslaved persons, and marginalized groups are largely absent from the historical record. Colonial-era scholarship on India and Africa often reflects European assumptions about 'progress' and 'civilization' that modern historians have critiqued. The sources for 1795 are generally reliable, but they reflect the perspectives and biases of their authors and the societies that produced them.

graph_edges.note: The Treaty of Basel with Prussia and the Treaty of Basel with Spain were signed in the same year and reflected similar strategic dynamics
The Third Partition of Poland followed the failure of the Kościuszko Uprising
The consolidation of the Thermidorian Reaction and British naval dominance were contemporary developments
The Jay Treaty's ratification in 1795 set the stage for the 1796 presidential election
Toussaint Louverture's consolidation of power in 1795 led to Haitian independence in 1804
